facts_disclosed: Quit ballet — switched to CrossFit for the intensity

turns.content: you know what's weird? I was walking past my old ballet studio today and I felt... nothing. like zero pull to go back in. that used to be the most important part of my week
That's an interesting moment of realization. When did you stop going? And do you think the "nothing" feeling means you've moved on, or that something shifted in what you need?

I stopped... maybe five, six months ago? it wasn't a dramatic decision. it just died. my schedule at the restaurant killed it. I was doing ballet twice a week — had been dancing since I was a kid in Russia — but I kept missing class because of double shifts. you can't do ballet inconsistently, your body loses it fast. so I'd miss a week, then two weeks, then a month, and eventually I just stopped pretending I was going to go back

That sounds like a gradual loss rather than a choice — the schedule made it impossible to maintain the consistency ballet demands. Dancing since childhood is a long relationship to let go of. Do you miss anything specific about it, even if you don't feel pulled to go back?

I miss the discipline of it sometimes. the way your body has to be precise and controlled, every muscle doing exactly what it's supposed to. that part was like cooking actually — the precision, the repetition until it's perfect. but I found something else that gives me what I need now

CrossFit. I started going to a box near the restaurant after I stopped ballet, and putain, it hit different. ballet was beautiful but CrossFit is... honest? like the bar doesn't care about your grace. either you can lift it or you can't. there's no pretending. I love that. after spending all night in a kitchen where everything is performance and presentation, I need something that's just raw effort

That's a really compelling distinction — ballet as performance and precision versus CrossFit as raw, unfiltered effort. It sounds like CrossFit gives you an outlet that's the opposite of what the kitchen demands. How often are you going?

four or five times a week now. which is more than I ever did ballet honestly. I think that tells you something. with ballet it was always this obligation — I should go, I need to go, I've been dancing since I was five I can't just stop. but CrossFit? I WANT to go. I wake up on my day off and I'm excited to go get destroyed by a workout. that's never happened to me with exercise before

Going from "I should go" to "I can't wait to go" is a huge shift — that kind of intrinsic motivation is hard to find. The fact that you're going four or five times a week versus the twice-a-week ballet schedule says a lot about how much it resonates with you. What kind of workouts do you gravitate toward?

the heavy stuff. deadlifts, cleans, squats. I like the barbell work more than the cardio pieces honestly. there's something about picking up something heavy that empties your brain completely. after a set of heavy deadlifts I can't think about Marc-Andre's cost spreadsheets or the new cook who keeps overcooking the fish or anything. it's just me and the weight and silence in my head for once

That mental silence is something a lot of people chase and never find — the fact that heavy lifting does that for you makes total sense given how much noise your work life carries. It sounds like CrossFit is serving a different emotional purpose than ballet did. Ballet was discipline and beauty; CrossFit is release and clarity.

exactly. and you know what, the physical pain is different too. after ballet my feet would ache and my hips would be tight and it felt like wear and tear, like my body was slowly breaking down from something elegant. after CrossFit my muscles are screaming but it feels like I built something. like the pain means I got stronger. it's more honest. blyat, my legs are always destroyed though

"Pain that means you built something" versus "pain from slow elegant breakdown" — that's a really perceptive way to describe it. One is constructive, the other was maintenance of something that was already slipping away. It sounds like quitting ballet wasn't really a loss once you found something that fits your life and your personality better.

non, it wasn't a loss. I think I held onto ballet for years out of loyalty to the girl I used to be, the little Russian girl in St. Petersburg in her dance shoes. but I'm not her anymore. I'm a woman who works brutal hours in a kitchen and needs to throw heavy things around to feel alive. c'est la vie. no regrets

That's a really honest way to frame it — letting go of who you were to embrace who you are now. The little girl in St. Petersburg led you here, and the woman you've become found what she actually needs. No regrets sounds right. CrossFit seems like it was waiting for you.
hah maybe. ok I need to go foam roll because tomorrow is back squats and if I show up tight my coach will lecture me for twenty minutes. he's worse than a chef, I swear. bonne nuit

Go foam roll — your coach and your back squats will thank you. It sounds like you've found something that genuinely fits your life, and that's worth celebrating. Bonne nuit, and good luck with the squats tomorrow.
